sentences.text: Yes.

First of all, I have to tell you that we had contact with an organization, a student organization, in Amsterdam who picked children out of the theater where they were brought together separate from the parents.
And he-- when he walked around in the morning, they picked a couple of kids out of there.
And-- and so the guards couldn't see that.
And [?
I think ?] [? they had ?] seven or eight kids they brought them to Lindberg, too.
And I picked them up and brought them to [?
John ?] [?
Bosch. ?] And he looked for hiding places for those kids.
And after the war, of course, you were registered by the Red Cross, all these kids.
And they were united with the parents.
A little girl we had at home, the parents knocked at our door once, and they came to pick up little Rachel.
And [INAUDIBLE] my parents were more or less disappointed.
And the reason why they were [INAUDIBLE] that's the little girl.
They were very attached to the little girl.
So it was heartbreaking when have to-- when they have to say goodbye.
And those parents were very understandable.
And they said, well, listen she shared for a year.
Our parents died in camp.
And we have no relatives anymore.
Can we adopt you as grandparents?
And that's what they did.
So after that, my mom and my father visited her and the people in Amsterdam where Rachel was.
And the first big holiday, Rachel was allowed to stay at our place, you know.
And there was a ?] fantastic, good relationship with that.
And [? we ?] would call them grandpa and grandma.
It was like [? being ?] in our family.
How long was that little girl with your parents?
Till the end of the war-- till September 1944.
Two or three years, I believe-- something like that, yeah, two or three years, yeah.
Well, she was crying, of course, in the beginning.
But we comfort her and say, come back.
And you know, Grandma will be here.
So that way we-- there was no problem.
Yes, the little girl was later on immigrated with the family to Israel.
And there she married and served there [? in the ?] Israeli army.
And she was involved in a car accident, was killed in a car accident, yeah.
So I met her [INAUDIBLE] daughter when I was in Israel, yeah.
Oh, short-- shortly after when they immigrated.
I thought ?] maybe in beginning of 1950, something like that.
She was 18 or 19 years of age.
That was when I was in Israel.
I met her daughter, yes.
Yes.
There are lots of children, of course, who had no parents anymore.
They were murdered in the Auschwitz or whatever.
But one guy, for example, he stood by the name of [?
Lody ?] [?
Weinbergen. ?] He was in concentration camp with his parents in the northern part of Holland.
And he got very seriously sick.
So he had to go to a hospital in the city of Groningen.
And his parents, they smuggled in [INAUDIBLE] to one of the [INAUDIBLE] a chance to-- to steal him out of the hospital.
They give permission for that.
So we went to this hospital.
And had a nurse-- we found a nurse we knew already who had opportunity to take him out of the hospital in Groningen.
And my sister later on picked him up, and we found a hiding place for him in Maastricht, neighborhood [? from ?] Maastricht.
And after the war, of course, his parents didn't come back.
But they found it better that he was brought up in a Jewish community.
So they brought him to a Jewish orphan home in Amsterdam.
Then he grew up, and then he immigrated to Israel.
And his [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] adopted parents were very [INAUDIBLE] angry about that, because he was attached to the boy.
But he came back later and did everything for them, you know.
He paid to put them to Israel, and he visited them several times.
And when those people died a couple of years ago, I believe they had no children.
Their only child was that Jewish boy.
In the will, the house was for him.
He got that house there.
Yeah.
Very attached.
Right.
He was around 5-years-old. 4, 5-years-old.
Yes.
Till the end of-- till the end of the end of the war.
Till the beginning-- the second half of 1945, when they took him from the [INAUDIBLE] to an orphan home, yeah.
No.
This particular boy-- there were people who didn't want to give him up, in particular, because his parents didn't come back.
And it became a court case.
And they just ordered him to go back to the Jewish community.
It was difficult for them, yeah.
People that had no children, of course they were attached to him.
Yeah.
Probably most of them, they understood the circumstances, because, you know, if you want to live a normal life, you have to act normal.
But there was never the problem, no.
I mean, you cannot make a person Christian by saying they're Christian.
I mean, it had to come in your heart.
[INAUDIBLE] So it didn't make any sense.
It's something that came from within.
Yes.
There was-- when I came back out of concentration camp, I was approached by the local police force.
And they asked me if I was willing to join them, especially to investigate political prisoners.
And I admit to that.
So I became part of the force.
I interrogated Nazi people, but also Germans who were-- were Gestapo agents and so on.
And one of them was Richard Nitch who interrogated me in-- when I was arrested.
He was one of the guys.
Yes.
And we had to go, and he shot my friend, Drik Van Assen.
And he had to go with this to point where he was buried.
Yeah.
He had no other way, because he was a prisoners, you know.
So we had him in a car.
And when the people in that part of Lindberg where most farmers live heard who he was, they came with their tools running to the car.
They want to hit him.
We had to protect him.
Yes.
It was a very hot day.
And everybody [? on turn ?] to go to into a restaurant to have a cool drink.
And I went in the restaurant for a cool drink, and he came out with another one for him.
And he give him that, and he said-- he looked at me.
And he bent his hat.
I said, what you do that for?
He said, I'm a Christian.
I want to show you that we don't hate.
And well, I guess there he was more [INAUDIBLE] than anything else that we expected there.
But he was later on-- he kept fighting a 20 years sentence.
And a couple of years ago, he was released and sent back to Germany.
Oh, roughly maybe 20, 30 years at least here.
Yeah.
In jail that long, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah.
In-- in the city, Breda-- B-R-E-D-A.
In the southern part of Holland there was a big prison, and he was-- he was there in that prison, yeah.
Yes.
The people who were-- were the traitor, [?
Vosenhaut. ?] He was also in court, and I was a witness there, too, to give testimony to what he did.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
He was also convicted to, I guess, 10 or 15 years in prison, yeah.
Most of the time he did.
Yes.
He was released, yes, yeah.
I don't know.
I lost track of him.
No.
Never.
No.
No.
No.
No.
Well, I left home.
I make it into Canada, because it was dangerous there.
They had a lot of people [? out of ?] the underground.
And the only thing they spoke about if you met them was what happened in the past.
And I'm not the type that's always looked to the past.
I like to look in the future.
So I got sick and tired of that.
And I decided with my wife to immigrate to Canada.
I start a new life here, and that we did.
And we ended up in Ottawa, and I'm still living here.
We arrived here in 1958.
Some people mentioned South Africa, but I was against the apartheid, so we didn't go there.
My brother lived in Australia, and I found it too far away to visit my parents once in a while.
So I thought the closest way was Canada.
And if you need [? help, ?] you had no problems.
Not necessarily because the southern part from Holland was liberated with the Americans.
You know, so I mean, they-- they didn't know anything about the Canadians.
That was only in the northern part, yeah.
Not much.
I used to [INAUDIBLE] before.
You know, I started my own business here in Ottawa.
I am an insurance broker.
The only thing that I find that I have an obligation now to tell other people, especially younger generation, what happened during war time as a witness.
And I like to, you know, especially with those people [INAUDIBLE] coming up about there was no Holocaust, I can tell them, [? the kids ?] [? that's ?] the biggest lie ever I have heard.
So that ?] [? there is ?] the truth.
And I get an opportunity to go to schools, and I'm registered in the Jewish community here to-- if they ask for a resource, then they call me sometimes, ask me to go to a school and talk to the kids.
The particular reason was, of course, our commitment as a Christian to help those people in need.
And at that particular time, [? we ?] [? wanted the ?] Jewish.
And for that reason, we help them.
No.
Not at all, no.
No.
I am glad what I did, but I always feel a little bit sorry that I couldn't do more.

Especially, if you read all the figures [? on ?] [? all those ?] people who were murdered and never came back [? in that era. ?] And we had cases that, for example, we [? worked ?] with a teacher from the high school.
He approached me, and he had a Jewish family-- father, mother, and four kids.
And they were desperate to find a hiding place, because they expected to be picked up the same day.
I found some people willing to put them in their homes.
So I was visiting people with him.
And they were putting all the clothes together.
And when we were there sitting, the bell rings, and it was the Dutch police force.
They are-- and on behalf of the Germans picked them up.
[INAUDIBLE] we have to pick them up.
And we asked the police man if he was willing to go away for half an hour.
He said, I'm sorry, but I have to do my duty.
And those four, those six people-- man, wife, and four kids-- they all came, and they all died in camp-- in concentration camp, yeah.
Well, first of all, you never plan to do those things, you know.
They-- they come-- instantly come up.
And I would think I would do the same thing, yeah, yeah.
No.
No.
Here's a person who did his ordinary duties.
That's all.
Yeah.
Yes.
Quite a few.
They are-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had not the conviction that [? we ?] [? are ?] [? here, ?] and that we're here to help each other.
And they always thought about themselves.
Didn't, you know-- they were afraid.
And that might happen.
That might happen.
And if all the people would have helped the Jewish, there was no Jew who would have ended up in camp.
That's my conviction, yeah.
Out of fear.
Yeah.
That's right.
Yeah, I must admit, of course, that I was a bachelor at the time.
I didn't have a family.
Maybe if you have a wife and kids you would maybe do it in a different ways.
I don't know.
Yes.
All of them were grown up, of course.
And my sister was at home, and she helped fully with that.
And my parents were fully involved in the undergrounds.
Have you been recognized by some organizations for the work that you've done?
Yes.
I was recognized for Yad Vashem.
What is Yad Vashem?
That is the place in Israel where they remember the people who are murdered in the Holocaust.
And I was there on two times.
And they have a tree planted there in the Yad Vashem.
And I got a medal from the Israeli government.
I got also a medal from the Dutch government.
And I got also an honorary doctorate degree from the university-- Lawrence University in Canton, New York.
That's the way they recognized me and all the people who were involved, yeah.
Yes.
That's right.
And then I was recognized some years ago at a Jewish community in Ottawa.
And they had an evening in a synagogue in the West End.
And they recognized me there.
They present-- it was a large gathering.
There was about 700, 800 people there.
And they [INAUDIBLE] from the speakers.
And I got-- they had-- going to open up a Holocaust resource center, and it's named after me.
In Ottawa in one of the schools here, in one of the buildings here.
Yeah.
Mendel Good is a Jewish fellow, a wealthy gentleman, who came out of concentration camp.
And he goes with me to the schools, you know.
And he tells his story from his side, and I tell the story from the Christian side, yes.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yeah.
Yeah.
Well, I-- I believe it is-- if you do this thing that makes you happy, if you give other people and forget about yourself, that makes you more happy than the other way around, yeah.
Yes.
Yes.
I gave them all-- all my kids, grandchildren, I gave them a copy of my book.
And as soon as they are able to read it, then the parents will give them the book, so they can read it.
[INAUDIBLE] parent [? can validate, ?] yeah.
It was Mr.
Dirk Van Assen.
And he audited in the income tax department.
And he was also an elder in our church, [INAUDIBLE].
Self-sacrifice.
Well, to-- to look at the need for others and don't look at [? your ?] need [? first, you know. ?] And help people who are in need.

The message I have, and I tell also the children in school, is that the Holocaust was not only took place in the gas chambers, but it started in the heart of the people when they started to hate people for reasons of faith, or religion, or race.
And if you want to avoid the next Holocaust, we should fight our own self and love the people, even if they're Jewish, or colored people, whatever.
You're welcome.
Thank you.
Yes.
Sure.
I would like to introduce to you my wife, Annette Van Mansum.
I met her some years ago in Toronto.
She's my second wife, and I'm her second husband.
And I was a widower, and she was a widow.
And we married in Toronto 21 years ago.
And she moved from Chicago to Ottawa, and now we live together in Ottawa.
And we're married for 21 years.
Yes.
This photo is made during when I was honored by the Jewish community in Masstricht in an upper room in the synagogue, in the local synagogue.
And [? there ?] was given to me a painting done by a famous artist, [?
Charles Icke ?] and listed with all the names of people I helped during the German wartime, yeah.
It was taken in the end of 1946.
I believe it was around November 1946.
There was Mr.
Tugentauf ?] standing there.
There was the rabbi of Maastricht was standing there.
And Mr.
Slyon ?] who was later on immigrated to Israel, yeah.
Yes.
To the right is my father and besides her is my mother.
And standing beside my mother and being sat by me is Fritz Freilich, who was hidden at the place till the end of the war there.
Also in the November 1945.
In Maastricht.
Yeah.
That's also in the synagogue when they had me there, gave me the painting during the celebration with the Jewish community there.
This is Fritz Freilich.
He-- the picture was taken after the war, in 1945.
He was hidden at our place during the wartime.
This picture is also made during the synagogue in Maastricht in the upper room there in November, 1945.
And there was a girl, the daughter of Mr.
And Mrs.
Wesley, [?
Lanie ?] Wesley, who handed me over some flowers.
And beside-- standing her mother and her father, Mr.
And Mrs.
Wesley and also Mr.
Frank ?].
He helped [? me ?] in wartime.
And this police officer from Maastricht who also was sometimes involved in helping me.
That's right and, she's now living under the name of Mrs.
Forgas.
And she lives in Tel Aviv.
Yes.
The photo was made in 1941.
We were required by the Germans to carry ID cards.
And that is it on one side of the ID card with a stamp on the city of Ottawa on top of it.
The city of?
Ottawa.
Sorry, the city of Maastricht.
That's the genuine item.
That's right, yes.
That's the flip side of this ID card.
We made two fingerprints.
One on the back of the picture and the other one separate.
And with my address, and every time you changed your address, you had to go to City Hall and change it.
This is genuine.
Yes.
Right.
Yes.
Yes.
This is a fake identity card.
It is [INAUDIBLE] on somebody who lives in Belgium.
And I went-- the police officer had an artist in the police office in Maastricht.
He took off his picture, put my picture on that, and copied the stamp, hand-copied the stamp.
And I used that card when I traveled to Belgium once in a while to [? help ?] also people hiding in Brussels or had to bring over somebody to Belgium.
I used this card.
I used ID card.
This is a forgery, yes.
Yes.
These are the food coupons we had to obtain every Monday, [? a sheet. ?] And then the papers announce what kind of number it was in that particular week.
We had to use for buying either meat, or milk, or bread, until all the numbers were gone, and they had to pick up a new sheets with numbers.
Yes.
This is a release form I got from the Germans when I was released from concentration camp.
It must be the second one, I suppose, yeah.
Yeah, that was the second one when I was arrested for the second time.
Concentration camp Amersfoort.
Yes.
There's a copy of it illegal magazine, Free Netherlands, that I was the provincial representative for this organization.
I spread the magazine around.
That's right.
Yes.
On the screen at the moment is the diploma of honor that my sister Margeretha received when Yad Vashem, because she so helped me in hiding Jewish and looking after them.
And she received this.
And that's correct, yes, in Jerusalem.
That was, I believe, it was 1962.
Before that she already had tree planted.
And it was one of the first trees [? she ?] [? get ?] Yad Vashem is for her.
Yes, that's right.
Yes.
That is what my sister received in Yad Vashem when she planted the tree.
I believe it was done in 1963.
She was one of the first, yeah.
Her tree is one of the biggest ones.
It's standing in front of the Lane of the Righteous Gentiles.
The medal I received in Yad Vashem-- that meant I was honored there.
I plant them a tree, and I got also this medal.
Yes.
That is.
Well, there's a well-known [INAUDIBLE] who developed.
I know the saying.
Well, one person helped the whole world-- something like that.
Yeah.
This certificate I got at the Israeli embassy in Ottawa in November, 1969, also given out by Yad Vashem.
Yes.
This certificate I received from the Jewish community in Ottawa in 1991 in January when they honored me in one of the synagogues in Ottawa.
I received this certificate in 1945 in November, when I was honored by the Jewish community in Maastricht.
And that meant the names of the people I was able to help during the wartime and who came alive out of the wartime.
[INAUDIBLE]
These are there-- not all, a few of them are missing, but the majority of the people I helped during the wartime.
Yes, this portrait was made by a well-known-- Dutch painter, Charles [DUTCH] And that was handed to me when I was celebrated and honored by the Jewish community in Maastricht in November of 1945.
There's my daughter, Margaret, my youngest daughter, with her husband, Rich, and their three children.
It was taken last year in Canada.
And [? now ?] my grandchildren are living in Canada, yeah.
Yes.
That's my only son, Gerrit with his wife, Hetje and the two children, who are living now in Dallas, Texas, in the United States.
No, the picture was taken just before they left.
Last year, yeah.
This is my oldest daughter, Ria,s and her husband, Ralph, and their four children.
They are all living in Canada.
In last year, 1995, in Canada.
